topic: Eclipse magnetic ensemble (39 eclipses) — priority-new review of analyst draft (EXP-038)
review_type: priority-new — reviewing analyst's proposed wins.json entry from EXP-038, not yet committed
our_argument_summary: The analyst proposes verdict 'Std Model Explains.' The argument: Kim & Chang (2018) is real science confirming eclipse geomagnetic effects, but the Chapman (1933) ionospheric conductivity mechanism has explained this for 93 years. The dome offers no derivation of magnitude/direction/timing from cavity geometry. The dome itself concedes non-discrimination at Tier 1. WIN-068 triple-counts the same phenomenon as WIN-010 and WIN-025.

relevant_files: raw-text/08-live-power.txt (Eclipse Magnetic Anomaly domain — PENDING for Aug 2026)
raw-text/02-wins.txt (PROS-003 — Eclipse Magnetic Anomaly Tracks Geometry)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: The dome's monitoring system has an 'Eclipse Magnetic Anomaly (Aug 12, 2026)' domain listed as PENDING with 128 days remaining. This is a FUTURE prediction domain for the 2026 eclipse, not a monitoring check for the historical 39-eclipse ensemble. The historical ensemble claim (WIN-068) has no monitoring pipeline at all — it's a retrospective literature citation, not a live-fetched or hardcoded check.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: No validation exists for WIN-068 specifically. The eclipse domain in monitor.py targets the 2026 event, not Kim & Chang's historical data.
data_sources_used: Kim & Chang 2018 (literature citation, not API fetch)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The dome's 'Steelman Evidence Ladder' places eclipse evidence at Tier 2, with the disclaimer that 'Tier 1 is consistent with both models.' The upcoming 2026 eclipse has a Kp < 2 precondition that voids the test ~50% of the time.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None — the analyst correctly identifies no monitoring pipeline for the historical ensemble claim.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: WIN-068 cites Kim & Chang (2018) historical data. The 'Eclipse Magnetic Anomaly' domain in the live power dashboard is a FUTURE prediction for Aug 2026, not a monitoring check for historical data. No API call, no hardcoded comparison, no fetch of Kim & Chang data. Tag: none.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Eclipse geomagnetic effects explained by Chapman (1933) ionospheric Sq current mechanism. The dome relabels this as 'firmament-plate dielectric boundary shading' without changing any numerical prediction. The dome's own PROS-003 description says 'the aetheric field is perturbed by the geometric shadow blocking the field interaction between sun and dome' — this is the Chapman mechanism with vocabulary substitution.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Kim & Chang (2018) published their 39-eclipse analysis covering 1991-2016. The dome model incorporated this as a WIN in V51.1 (April 2026). All 39 eclipses predate the dome's claim by years to decades. The dome's PROS-003 entry does claim prospective prediction for the eclipse magnetic pattern, but the specific '39 eclipses' ensemble is post-hoc adoption of published literature.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: The dome asserts 'aetheric field perturbation by geometric shadow' but provides no equation deriving the expected magnetic magnitude, component pattern, latitude dependence, or timing from dome geometry (disc radius, firmament height, etc.). The claim is qualitative assertion, not quantitative derivation.

holes_found.description: WRONG DOI for Chapman 1933. The proposed text links to DOI 10.1098/rsta.1933.0001 (Phil. Trans. Royal Society A), but Chapman's 1933 eclipse paper was published in 'Terrestrial Magnetism and Atmospheric Electricity' (now JGR), not Phil. Trans. The correct reference is Chapman (1933), Terr. Magn. Atmos. Electr. 38(3). Web search confirms the paper's correct venue. A wrong DOI for our single most-cited foundational reference (used across WIN-010, WIN-025, and WIN-068) would allow a dome defender to claim we fabricate citations — exactly the kind of error that discredits an entire review.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace the Chapman 1933 link with the correct DOI or a verified URL. Verify across WIN-010 and WIN-025 as well — if the same wrong DOI appears there, all three need fixing.

holes_found.description: Unverified dome quotes. The proposed text quotes the dome as having an 'honest caveat' that 'Globe has a competing ionospheric conductivity mechanism' and that 'Tier 1 is consistent with both models.' These phrases do not appear in our V51.0 raw-text captures. They appear to be from V51.1 content (the Steelman Evidence Ladder) that hasn't been captured in raw-text/ yet. If these are inaccurate paraphrases rather than exact quotes, we risk the strawman accusation.
holes_found.recommendation: Before committing WIN-068 to wins.json, verify these quotes against the actual V51.1 dome site text. If paraphrasing, use 'the dome acknowledges...' rather than quoting. Update raw-text/ with V51.1 captures.

holes_found.description: The 'Ashour-Chapman conductivity model' link goes to a Frontiers in Astronomy and Space Sciences paper (doi: 10.3389/fspas.2021.766327). While the link text says 'Ashour-Chapman conductivity model,' the Ashour-Chapman model is actually from Ashour (1950) / Chapman (1919), and the Frontiers paper is a more recent review. The link is not wrong per se, but naming it 'the Ashour-Chapman conductivity model' implies this Frontiers paper IS the model, when it's a 2021 review that discusses it. A pedantic defender could call this misleading citation practice.
holes_found.recommendation: Either link to the original Ashour (1950) paper or clarify that the Frontiers link is a modern review that applies the Ashour-Chapman framework. Consider citing as 'quantitative ionospheric models building on Chapman (1933) and Ashour (1950)' with appropriate links.

holes_found.description: detail_group inconsistency. WIN-010 and WIN-025 currently have detail_group 'WIN-010/025'. The proposed WIN-068 has detail_group 'WIN-010/025/068'. If WIN-068 is added without updating WIN-010 and WIN-025's detail_group to match, the grouping will be asymmetric — WIN-068 references WIN-010/025 but they don't reference it back.
holes_found.recommendation: When adding WIN-068 to wins.json, simultaneously update WIN-010 and WIN-025's detail_group to 'WIN-010/025/068'.

holes_found.description: The claim field 'Eclipse magnetic ensemble (39 eclipses)' is clear, but the finding field could be tighter. 'Kim & Chang 2018 confirmed eclipse geomagnetic effects — standard ionospheric Sq mechanism (Chapman 1933)' buries the triple-counting argument that is arguably our strongest point against this WIN. A reader scanning the summary table sees only this one line.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider revising the finding to lead with the triple-counting: 'Same phenomenon as WIN-010/025 at different aggregation; standard Sq mechanism (Chapman 1933) predicts all observations' or similar.

holes_found.description: The PROS-003 text in the dome's own wins page says the eclipse prediction was prospective: 'What we predicted: During a solar eclipse, a magnetic anomaly would appear...' The dome claims PROS-003 (made before some confirming data) maps to WIN-010/WIN-025. If the dome similarly claims PROS-003 covers WIN-068, then the post_hoc tag needs nuance. The qualitative prediction ('magnetic anomaly during eclipse') may have been prospective; the specific '39 eclipses' quantification is clearly post-hoc. Our text should distinguish between the qualitative claim (arguably prospective) and the quantitative confirmation (clearly post-hoc).
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentence acknowledging that the dome's eclipse magnetic prediction (PROS-003) may predate some confirming data, while noting that the specific Kim & Chang ensemble analysis was published years before V51.1 incorporated it. This shows intellectual honesty without conceding the WIN.

stronger_arguments.description: The dome's three eclipse WINs (010, 025, 068) use three different magnitude values: -10.9 nT (BOU 2017), ~-10 nT (9-station average 2024), and unstated (39-eclipse ensemble). The analyst's analysis-20260408-v51-1-update.json notes the dome itself has THREE different predicted magnitudes for the 2026 eclipse: homepage -17 to -21 nT, killshot.html -28.9 nT, code -29.1 nT. This internal inconsistency in eclipse magnitude predictions is an untapped self-contradiction argument that could strengthen the WIN-068 entry.

The Kim & Chang (2018) paper found that eclipse geomagnetic effects are strongest at magnetic latitudes 30°-50°. This latitude dependence is a direct prediction of the Sq current mechanism (the Sq current vortex has maximum current density at ~30° magnetic latitude). The dome model provides no prediction for this latitude dependence from its geometry. This is a missed discriminating test: standard physics predicts WHERE the effect is strongest, the dome doesn't.
sources: Kim & Chang 2018, Advances in Space Research 61:2040-2054

kernel_of_truth.description: The analyst's kernel of truth is well-stated: Kim & Chang (2018) IS excellent peer-reviewed science, the ensemble approach IS methodologically superior to single-event studies, and the dome model DOES correctly identify the phenomenon. The dome's willingness to cite real papers and acknowledge competing explanations (at Tier 1) shows greater intellectual engagement than typical flat-earth claims. The decision to expand from single-event to ensemble evidence shows awareness of the importance of statistical power.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The analyst correctly identifies: Chapman mechanism predates the dome by 93 years, the dome provides no quantitative derivation, and the dome concedes non-discrimination. The ensemble aggregation doesn't create new physics — it's the same ionospheric conductivity mechanism at higher statistical power. Higher statistical power for an effect that standard physics already explains doesn't discriminate between models.

deeper_analysis:
The verdict 'Std Model Explains' is correct. The analyst's draft is solid in substance but has one major citation error (Chapman DOI) and several moderate issues (unverified dome quotes, Ashour-Chapman link mislabeling, detail_group inconsistency) that should be fixed before committing to wins.json.

The most valuable missed opportunity is the eclipse magnitude inconsistency: the dome's own predictions show three different values for the 2026 eclipse (-17 to -21 nT on homepage, -28.9 nT on killshot.html, -29.1 nT in code). This inconsistency, combined with the fact that the historical data shows ~10 nT, means the dome's 'prediction' for the ensemble magnitude is unstated — it simply observes that the effect exists without quantifying it. This is the definition of a non-prediction.

The triple-counting argument is strong and well-made. WIN-010, WIN-025, and WIN-068 all cite eclipse geomagnetic perturbation at different scales. Counting the same physical phenomenon three times (single station, 9 stations, 39 eclipses) inflates the WIN count. The analyst should consider whether this triple-counting pattern exists elsewhere in the 69-WIN list — it would make a good cross-cutting argument for the holistic review phase.

One concern: the proposed text says the dome's discriminator (contact-phase timing) is 'directly contradicted' by Kim & Chang showing peak at eclipse maximum. Verified: Kim & Chang do show the effect over a ~180-minute window centered on eclipse maximum. However, the dome's contact-phase claim appears to be about a different prediction (E-PRED-A for 2026), not necessarily about the historical ensemble. The analyst should be careful not to conflate the dome's FUTURE prediction criteria with its PAST confirmation claims. If the dome says contact-phase timing is the discriminator for 2026 (not for the historical ensemble), then using it against WIN-068 is a strawman. The text should clarify which dome claim it's responding to.

citations_verified: Kim & Chang (2018, Advances in Space Research 61:2040-2054) — VERIFIED via web search. Paper exists, correct journal/volume/pages, confirms 39 eclipses 1991-2016, 100 INTERMAGNET stations, 207 manifestations.
INTERMAGNET reference — VERIFIED, intermagnet.org is the correct URL for the international network.
citations_failed: Chapman (1933) DOI 10.1098/rsta.1933.0001 — FAILED. Chapman's 1933 eclipse paper was in 'Terrestrial Magnetism and Atmospheric Electricity' 38(3), not Phil. Trans. Royal Society A. The DOI resolves to a different paper. Correct reference: Chapman, S. (1933). 'The effect of a solar eclipse on the Earth's magnetic field.' Terr. Magn. Atmos. Electr. 38(3).

Ashour-Chapman conductivity model link (10.3389/fspas.2021.766327) — PARTIAL FAIL. The link likely resolves to a legitimate Frontiers paper but it's a 2021 review, not the original Ashour-Chapman model (Ashour 1950). The text implies this IS the model rather than a paper discussing it.
citations_unchecked: The specific Frontiers link (10.3389/fspas.2021.766327) — could not verify content in this run but the DOI format is valid for Frontiers in Astronomy and Space Sciences.

advocate_mode.best_defense: A dome defender who has read our six methodological principles could argue: 'Your own Principle 3 says no default favoritism — both models must earn their explanations. You dismiss WIN-068 as Std Model Explains, but the standard model's Sq current explanation has its own problems: it requires precisely the E-region conductivity profile that happens to produce ~10 nT at ground level, and Chapman's 1933 model was semi-quantitative at best. Modern ionospheric models still disagree on the exact magnitude by factors of 2-3 for any given eclipse (see Malin et al. 1986, Takeda & Araki 1984). So when you say the standard model quantitatively predicts these observations, you're overstating the precision of the standard prediction. Both models qualitatively predict the phenomenon; neither precisely derives the magnitude from first principles for any arbitrary eclipse.' This is a fair critique — our text says the Ashour-Chapman model 'quantitatively predicts' things, but the standard model's eclipse predictions are better described as 'qualitatively consistent with the mechanism' rather than 'quantitatively precise.'
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Soften 'quantitatively predicts the magnitude, timing, and component-dependence' to something like 'predicts the qualitative pattern: Y-increase, X/Z/F-decrease, peak at eclipse maximum, strongest at Sq-vortex latitudes. The specific magnitudes vary by eclipse geometry and ionospheric conditions, but the standard mechanism correctly predicts the sign, component structure, and timing without invoking any dome geometry.' This is more accurate and harder to attack.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: No logical inconsistencies between our arguments. WIN-010 (BOU 2017), WIN-025 (2024 9-station), and proposed WIN-068 (39-eclipse ensemble) all use the Chapman mechanism argument consistently. WIN-011 (Mohe gravity) and WIN-012 (coupling constant) are about the gravity component, not the magnetic component, so they don't overlap. One PRESENTATION inconsistency: WIN-010 has monitoring='hardcoded' while WIN-025 has monitoring='none' and proposed WIN-068 has monitoring='none'. This is correct (WIN-010's domain exists as a hardcoded check in monitor.py, while WIN-025 and WIN-068 have no monitoring), but a defender might ask why the same phenomenon gets different monitoring treatments. Our code analysis section should note this — it reflects the dome's selective automation, not a flaw in our review.

claims_checked: 93 years between Chapman 1933 and dome V51.1 2026 → 93 years. Correct.
Kim & Chang paper details: 39 eclipses, 100 stations, 207 manifestations, 1991-2016 → all verified against paper metadata.
180-minute window centered on eclipse maximum → consistent with Kim & Chang methodology.
quantitative_verification.computations: No complex mathematical claims in this WIN — it's primarily a mechanism-attribution argument (standard physics vs dome physics). The numerical claims are about paper metadata (39 eclipses, 100 stations) and timeline (93 years), all verified.
